text.title: terraform-modules-versioning Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for terraform-modules-versioning. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Major version bumps (X.0.0) MUST be used for any change that requires consumers to update variable names, remove previously supported variables, or change output names. A migration guide MUST accompany every major release.
rule.rationale: Without a clear signal, consumers cannot distinguish a bug fix from a breaking change at a glance. Major bumps give teams a predictable gate for planning upgrades.

rule.statement: Minor version bumps (0.X.0) MUST be used for new optional variables, new outputs, or new resources that are backward-compatible. Existing callers MUST continue to work without modification.
rule.rationale: Consumers who rely on ~> constraints (see below) will pick up minor versions automatically. Breaking backward compatibility in a minor bump violates consumer expectations.

rule.statement: Patch version bumps (0.0.X) MUST be used for bug fixes that do not add new features or change behavior. No consumer code changes required.
rule.rationale: Patch bumps signal safe auto-adoption for consumers using pessimistic constraints.

rule.statement: All module source references that include a version argument MUST use the pessimistic constraint operator (~>) to allow patch and minor updates within a major version, or an explicit range. The exact-pin operator (=) MUST only be used when a specific patch is known to fix a critical bug.
rule.rationale: ~> 5.0 allows 5.0.1, 5.1.0, 5.9.9 but blocks 6.0.0 — safe for routine updates while blocking breaking changes. Exact pins require manual bumps for every patch release, increasing maintenance burden.

rule.statement: Registry and GitHub module sources MUST always include the version argument. Local path sources (./modules/X) MUST NOT include a version argument.
rule.rationale: Local modules have no separate release lifecycle — the version argument is ignored and its presence misleads maintainers.

rule.statement: Every published module MUST maintain a CHANGELOG.md in the module root. Each release MUST document: the version number, release date, and categorized changes (Added, Changed, Breaking Changes, Fixed, Deprecated, Removed). Breaking changes MUST be in their own section and include migration instructions.
rule.rationale: Consumers deciding whether to upgrade need to evaluate impact without reading the full diff. A categorized CHANGELOG reduces the upgrade decision from O(diff) to O(CHANGELOG section).
